coredata.dc:title: Commonly available, everyday materials as non-conventional powders for the visualization of latent fingerprints
coredata.dc:creator: Vadivel, Ramanan
Nirmala, Muthukumaran
Anbukumaran, Karthikeyani
coredata.dc:description: The fingerprint development technique is one of the oldest methods used in forensic science and is a common method of identifying perpetrators. Powder dusting is a popular method for detecting latent fingerprints. Our immediate need is to identify a cheap, non-toxic, commonly available powder as an alternative to expensive, toxic, complicated powders. The goal of this review is to compile the research works done by various experts to identify commonly available, everyday materials as fingerprint powders. The authors have discussed sixty four of such materials from various categories such as food powders, plant materials, minerals, cosmetics, carbon-based materials, and miscellaneous powders. Also they have summarized the key points of the articles that have been published in the context of the goal. Among the various everyday used powders, turmeric powder was identified as a promising material for the detection of latent fingerprints. The effectiveness of white cement, silica gel G, gram flour, etc. was also impressive. Chilli-based powders have been reported to be poor fingerprint powders. Luminescent carbon dots are fascinating newcomers to this field but they have not yet been investigated as a dedicated fingerprint powder. This area provides ample opportunity for researchers to explore the ability of various commonly available, non-toxic and inexpensive, everyday materials to be applied as non-conventional fingerprint powders.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fingerprint development technique is one of the oldest methods used in forensic science and is a common method of identifying perpetrators. Powder dusting is a popular method for detecting latent fingerprints. Our immediate need is to identify a cheap, non-toxic, commonly available powder as an alternative to expensive, toxic, complicated powders. The goal of this review is to compile the research works done by various experts to identify commonly available, everyday materials as fingerprint powders. The authors have discussed sixty four of such materials from various categories such as food powders, plant materials, minerals, cosmetics, carbon-based materials, and miscellaneous powders. Also they have summarized the key points of the articles that have been published in the context of the goal. Among the various everyday used powders, turmeric powder was identified as a promising material for the detection of latent fingerprints. The effectiveness of white cement, silica gel G, gram flour, etc. was also impressive. Chilli-based powders have been reported to be poor fingerprint powders. Luminescent carbon dots are fascinating newcomers to this field but they have not yet been investigated as a dedicated fingerprint powder. This area provides ample opportunity for researchers to explore the ability of various commonly available, non-toxic and inexpensive, everyday materials to be applied as non-conventional fingerprint powders.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprints are the tiny ridges formed during the embryological development of the volar pads of the fingers, which allow one to grip surfaces without slipping. Although researchers have raised concerns about the uniqueness of fingerprints over time, they continue to be a popular method of distinguishing between different individuals – including identical twins [1,2]. Furthermore, the fingerprints are permanent and do not change over time. These characteristics make them valuable evidence at a crime scene. Crime investigation using fingerprints has been documented for over a century [3]. The revelation of latent fingerprints is based on either chemical reactions between the fingermark residues and chemical agents or physical adherence of the developing agents to the surface of the fingerprint residues. Both the mechanisms may be active at the same time in some cases.

Although several advancements have been made to detect the invisible fingerprints, the powder dusting method is the most traditional, with one of its earlier references [4] dating back to 1891. Pressure deficit is the major mechanism behind the adherence of particulates of fingerprint powder formulation to fingerprint emanations. Pressure deficit is caused by the curvature of meniscus generated by the wetting of the powder particles on the fingerprint residue (wetting provides a larger contact area for the powder), which causes the particle to adhere to the sweat deposits [5]. This phenomenon is also associated to the capillary force of the liquid, which is triggered by its surface tension [6]. In addition, the electrostatic attraction between the fingerprint residue and the powder particles caused by frictional charges contributes to adhesion, albeit in a minor way [7].

The efficiency of developing latent fingerprints with a fingerprint powder is determined by several factors, including particle morphology (small and fine particles adhere more easily than large and coarse particles), age of the fingerprint under investigation (the liquid content of the fingerprint residue decreases with time, reducing powder adsorption), ambient conditions such as temperature (elevated temperature and low pressure produce similar effects observed for aged fingerprints), the colour (colour of the fingerprint powder should be selected keeping the colour of the surface in mind in order to develop the latent fingerprints with good contrast) and nature of the surface (non-porous, non-textured surfaces are easy to handle than porous, textured surfaces) on which the latent fingerprint is deposited.

The primary reason for the need for alternative fingerprint powders is the numerous health hazards posed by conventional fingerprint powders during occupational exposure. Heavy metal toxicity, For example, is a significant health concern for forensic and law enforcement personnel who work with inorganic fingerprint powders. As a result, lead- and mercury-based powder formulations have become obsolete. Several studies have been published so far on the health hazards of fingerprint powders on occupational exposure [5,8,9]. Even common fingerprint powders such as cyanoacrylate fumes and carbon black powder have been linked to health risks [10,11]. Souter et al. [12] found a significant increase in skin disorders and visual disorders in police officers after occupational exposure to fingerprint powders.

Apart from toxicity threats, a good fingerprint powder formulation needs to satisfy some important criteria, which are listed as follows.
1.
The particles of the powder should be fine and small. Otherwise, it may prevent the exploration of important ridge pattern features, particularly the third level details.
2.
The constituents of the fingerprint powder should not be chemically or physically attracted to the surface being examined. This can be related to the sensitivity of the powder. For example, aluminium flakes are more sensitive than aluminium powder. However, sensitivity is not a crucial criterion in all cases.
3.
The fingerprint powder should offer good color contrast on all surfaces (this criterion is difficult to achieve because there is a possibility in which both fingerprint powder and the surface are the same color).
4.
The powder should possess a reasonable flowability and should not undergo caking which would prevent it from being used at all.
5.
The powder should be stable and should possess a long shelf life.
6.
The powder formulation should not contain any toxic ingredient.
7.
The powder should not be too expensive.
8.
It is preferable if the powder is formulated from readily available common ingredients. Complicated ingredients may be difficult to obtain, which may limit their use in practical applications.
9.
The powder formulation should not be clumsy (like graphite powder).

It is very difficult to formulate a universal fingerprint powder that meets all of the criteria listed above. As a result, thousands of powders have been formulated over time by various researchers. According to fingerprint experts, no single powder is appropriate for all cases involving different surfaces, conditions, and nature of the fingerprint under examination. Hence, each fingerprint should be dealt with separately. Therefore, selection of fingerprint powder for a particular fingerprint is crucial and involves diverse factors. In this context, one should have a wide variety of fingerprint powders to apply on various crime scene fingerprints, which justifies the importance of identifying various new fingerprint powder formulations.

Despite the fact that thousands of fingerprint formulations have been reported, the development of fingerprint powders still has a lot of scope since we need to overcome the toxicity, high-cost, and scarcity of conventional fingerprint powders.

In this connection, many unconventional fingerprint powders have been introduced by various research groups. Most of them are chemical compounds such as organic dyes. In the book, “Sign Out Science: Simple Hands-on Experiments using Everyday Materials” published in 1993, Carlette et al. [13] demonstrated a simple hands-on experiment to reveal latent fingerprints using talcum powder as an everyday material. To date, talcum powder is only used for demonstration purposes and not as a dedicated fingerprint powder. Graphite powder also falls under this category [14]. However, in 2011, Garg’s research group [15] introduced turmeric powder as an efficient fingerprint powder. Turmeric powder is a common food ingredient as well as an important home medicine. It is easily available, cheap, and non-toxic. The latent fingerprints developed with turmeric powder yielded excellent results. In the same year, the same research group published [16] another article demonstrating the decipherment of latent fingerprints using common food powders and festival colors. The results were impressive. Following that, many research groups began to investigate the ability of various household and commonly available powders to be used in the field of forensic fingerprinting. Recently, Passos et al. [17] applied an algal biomass powder to reveal latent fingerprints on glass surfaces.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first review that covers articles published on the application of commonly available powders to reveal latent fingerprints. This could be extremely beneficial to researchers working to investigate various non-conventional powders. We hope that this review will inspire researchers to identify many commonly available powders for fingerprinting applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: What has been covered and what has been left out of this review?
ce:section.ce:para: This review focuses on articles published between 2011 and 2020 on the application of various non-conventional, commonly available powders to reveal latent fingerprints. Some of the plant materials were heated before being used as fingerprint powder. We considered these materials as carbon powders and discussed them under the heading “carbon-based materials”. Finally, we briefly discussed carbon dots, an emerging luminescent material that is now being used as a luminescent fingerprint powder. Carbon dots are not a common everyday material but they can be easily obtained even from waste materials around us using a simple household microwave oven [18,19]. At the same time, we didn’t go into detail about the use of carbon dots to detect latent fingerprints, as this has already been done elsewhere [20]. We took utmost care to include all relevant articles in this review. We sincerely apologize to the researchers whose important work has been overlooked.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Commonly available, everyday materials as non-conventional powders for the visualization of latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: We divided the common everyday materials applied for the detection of latent fingerprints into six categories namely, “food materials and food additives”, “plant materials”, “cosmetics”, “minerals”, “carbon-based materials” and ‘’miscellaneous”. The common everyday materials discussed in this review are summarized in tabular (Tables 2–7) form at the end of this review for the convenience of the readers.

Many research groups have demonstrated the application of various food materials and food additives that are easily available in homes in developing latent fingerprints, and this section elaborates on this.

Rohatgi et al. [21] reported their preliminary attempt to develop latent fingerprints using commonly used household kitchen food products such as cocoa powder (constitutes mainly of caffeine, theobromine, catechin, carbohydrates, proteins, flavonoids, polyphenols, and so on), cornflour (composed mainly of starch, a carbohydrate (polysaccharide) that contains 20–25% amylase and 75–80% amylopectin), custard powder (a powder containing corn-flour, egg yolk, sugar, etc.), baking soda (sodium bicarbonate, NaHCO3), baking powder (a mixture of sodium bicarbonate and cream of tartar), black salt (mainly composed of sodium chloride, NaCl), edible food color (orange color) and turmeric powder. The chemical structures of major constituents present in the cocoa powder and cornflour are depicted in Figs. 3 and 4 , respectively. According to their findings, cocoa powder and turmeric powder performed the best on all porous and non-porous surfaces tested, with the exception of rubber and cardboard. Negative results were obtained on white ceramic tile, transparent polythene and thermocol due to poor contrast in cases of custard powder, cornflour, baking soda, baking powder and black salt. These surfaces, on the other hand, had clear visible fingerprints with edible (orange) color and turmeric. No prints could be developed on rubber and cardboard due to the lack of adherence of sebum from latent fingerprints and developing agents on these surfaces.

The master degree thesis submitted by Yi et al. [24] discussed the use of turmeric and other spices such as cinnamon and paprika as powders to decipher latent fingerprints. Although cinnamon contains several chemical compounds, the active ingredient is cinnamaldehyde, which is responsible for its pungent taste and its characteristic aroma [26]. Another spice, paprika is widely used in food preparation since it contains many biologically active compounds [26,27]. Besides that, it is popular for its brilliant red color. The red color is imparted by the compounds capsanthin and capsorubin present in paprika [26]. Despite from the fact that other researchers have previously investigated the ability of turmeric powder as fingerprint powder [15,21–23], the thesis provides verification results of turmeric powder as fingerprint powder on various surfaces. Based on the demonstrated results, turmeric powder could be used effectively on non-porous surfaces for sebaceous fingerprints, but the results for eccrine fingerprints were not as good as those obtained using conventional carbon black powder. It was also found that the cinnamon powder provide clear and rich details of sebaceous fingerprints, particularly on non-porous surfaces. The paprika powder was found to be a poor material for fingerprint application, which is consistent with the findings of Seerat et al. [22]. The absence of resinous content in the paprika powder was cited as a possible reason for its poor adherence to oily and moisture components.

Chauhan et al. [29] conducted preliminary experiments to investigate the effectiveness of gram flour and coffee powder (consists of caffeine, carbohydrates, phenols, etc.) as fingerprint powders. The authors assigned success rates of 86.66% and 76.66% to coffee powder and gram flour, respectively. The authors claim that the developed prints by these powders are clear and reveal both the individual and class characteristics.

Many research groups have used powders derived from plant materials to detect latent fingerprints. Lycopodium spores are one of the classic conventional plant materials still in use today for the development of latent fingerprints, either applied as a sole material or in combination with other materials. This section discusses such plant materials for the decipherment of latent fingerprints.

An interesting work was done by King et al. [30] which demonstrated the application of Spirulina platensis powder as a strong near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent fingerprint powder. The ability of S. platensis to fluoresce in the NIR region is attributed to the presence of pigments, phycocyanins which are light-harvesting proteins found in many cyanobacteria, such as Spirulina. It absorbs orange-red light around 620–640 nm and fluoresces from 650 nm into the NIR region [30]. NIR powders have their own advantages over the conventional luminescent powders, which exhibit visible luminescence under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. It is often difficult to differentiate between the fingerprint and the substrate especially for fingerprints deposited on some polymers and paper materials, which fluoresce on the exposure of UV light. In this context, King et al. [30] successfully applied the Spirulina powder on polymer banknotes and magazine pages (Fig. 16 ) to decipher latent fingerprints. The study [30] also looked into the NIR emitting nature of wheatgrass, spinach, kale, lime, Chlorella, broccoli, and moringa powders. Among them, wheatgrass, spinach and moringa exhibited a significant reduction in luminescence above 850 nm.

King et al. [30] also investigated the efficiency of Spirulina as a stand-alone fingerprint powder and viewed the developed fingerprints either under white or halogen light, claiming that the green colour of the Spirulina powder exhibits good contrast on white, grey or black backgrounds. Later, Passos et al. [17] have extended the application of Spirulina powder on sebaceous fingerprints. They [17] characterized five different marine plant biomasses namely, Chlorella sp., Desmarestia anceps, Laurencia dendroidea, Lessonia searlesiana and Spirulina sp. and investigated their physicochemical properties in order to apply them as fingerprint developers. Among the marine plant materials investigated by the research group, Spirulina sp. produced the best results for the development of natural and sebaceous latent fingerprints.

A few research groups [29,31,32] have reported the application of henna powder (Lawsonia inermis) to reveal latent fingerprints. Henna is a natural plant that contains the red–orange pigment lawsone. The various tautomeric forms of lawsone are portrayed in Fig. 17 . During festivals and wedding celebrations, some populations such as Indians apply henna paste to their fingers and palms. The pigment lawsone is released only in acidic media. For this purpose, acidic food materials such as lemon or tamarind is added to the henna leaves and ground to a fine paste. Addition of sugar makes this paste sticky on the palms and fingers, enhancing the interaction between the stratum corneum, the outermost layer of the skin, and the lawsone pigment. The pigment will then migrate to the skin, where it will develop orange-red colouration. The diffusion of lawsone occurs deeply into the skin when the henna paste is applied for a long time. Some populations, such as Indians apply henna paste to their white hair to give it a redhead appearance. It’s no surprise that researchers are using henna to detect latent fingerprints because they want to take advantage of the natural interaction between the henna and the skin.

Sivaraj et al. [31] studied the use of lawsone in conjunction with black powder to enhance latent fingerprints on paper materials, which were then compared to traditional ninhydrin. In this work, one week and four months old fingerprints on various paper materials such as newspaper, envelope, writing paper and tissue paper were stained by standard ninhydrin solution, lawsone with the aid of black powder and the fraction obtained from henna containing lawsone with the aid of black powder. The results for ninhydrin stained fingerprints on new and one week old fingerprints on envelope and newspapers were appreciable, but the results for fingerprints deposited on writing paper and four months old fingerprints on any paper material under investigation were not. On the other hand, the lawsone and fraction containing lawsone with the aid of black powder produced clear results for both the new and old fingerprints deposited on all of the paper except the tissue paper. No staining material under investigation including the standard ninhydrin solution was able to reveal the latent fingerprints on tissue paper. It is obvious that the latent fingerprints on highly porous and absorbing surfaces often fail to offer clear fingerprints.

Later, Chauhan et al. [29] have demonstrated the development of latent fingerprints on A4 paper using natural henna. The authors state that the Lawsonia inermis reacts with amino acids and fatty acids present in sweat, resulting in deposits that are adsorbed by friction ridge residues and reveal the fingerprint pattern. The authors further state that natural henna has the ability to absorb atmospheric moisture and darken over time, usually within 30 min. However, it appears that this time is longer for practical applications.

Another research group, Anand et al. [32] examined the effectiveness of henna powder in developing latent fingerprints on various non-porous and porous substrates. The henna-developed prints were completely identifiable on foil paper and plastic substrates. On the other hand, they were partially identifiable on granite, leather, paper, and cardboard. The fingerprints developed on wooden surfaces were the least identifiable.

To our wonder, none of the above-mentioned studies to explore the ability of henna as a fingerprint powder used any acid in their formulations. They could have released the pigment lawsone more effectively, resulting in clear fingerprints.

Sekar et al. [33] developed a fingerprint powder from powdered durian seeds and tested it on various porous and non-porous surfaces. The authors claim that in most of the surfaces studied, the durian seed-derived powder revealed clear fingerprints. However, the second and third level details are not clearly visible in many developed fingerprints. Moreover, the white colour of the durian seed powder prevents it from being applied to light-colored surfaces due to poor contrast. The hydrogen bond formation between the fatty acids and glycerides of sebum and the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the components in durian seed powder has been assigned as the possible reason for the adherence of durian seed powder to fingerprints. Since the major constituent of durian seed is starch, it is no surprise that durian powder adheres to fingerprints, as starch is a well-known constituents (usually as an adhesive) in many fingerprint powder formulations.

Sari et al. [34] have devised gambir powder as a green fingerprint revealer. Besides being commonly used as a remedy to relieve inflammatory skin condition and headaches, gambir, ranging from yellow to brown, was also used as textile dyes and tanning agents [35]. The gambir powder developed well-defined fingerprints (CAST absolute scale: 4 (over 2/3 clear ridge detail) [36]) in various non-porous substrates such as aluminium foil, glass slide, plastic cup, compact disc and transparent plastic. These fingerprints allow for the extraction of all three levels of information. In addition, the authors also studied the influence of particle size on the adherence of gambir powder to fingerprint residues deposited on different non-porous substrates. The authors found that the relatively coarse gambir powder (0.250 mm) was better suited for fingerprints deposited on glass slides and transparent plastics, whereas the finer particles of 0.125 mm performed considerably better on plastic cups, compact discs, and aluminium foils.
Most of the cosmetics contain pigments that offer a possibility to employ them as fingerprint developers as discussed in this section.

Completely identifiable prints have been found in the cases of bleach activator and face pack development on many substrates under study (Entry 3 in Table 4). Completely and partially identifiable prints had been developed on many substrates that had been treated with face powder and pink eye shadow. The overall observation concluded that best results could be obtained for latent fingerprints deposited on aluminium foil sheet, while cardboard substrate produces the least identifiable prints.

Thakur et al. [39] have reported the multani mitti aka Fuller’s earth, to be an efficient fingerprint developing powder on various substrates. Fuller’s earth is composed of hydrous aluminium silicate in different compositions. It comes under montmorillonite ((Na,Ca)0.33(Al,Mg)2(Si4O10)(OH)2·nH2O) category with varying compositions of attapulgite [40]. Some other minerals such as calcite, dolomite, and quartz may also be present in fuller’s earth in small amounts. In some localities, fuller’s earth refers to calcium bentonite since its chemical and physical properties resemble calcium montmorillonite [41].

The Fuller’s earth developed fingerprints are clear and the minutiae features are easily identifiable in most of the cases (Entry 5 in Table 4). The Fuller’s earth develops the fingerprints with a good contrast compared with other conventional powders such as black, grey and magnum powder. It selectively adsorbs on ridge prints. The Fuller’s earth had been employed successfully on six-day-old latent fingerprints that had been kept in the shade. At the same time, the fingerprints exposed to sunlight develop only weak prints as time elapses. Fig. 20 depicts the fingermarks developed after 97 h of exposure to sunlight and shade with Fuller’s earth. The authors justified the observation of weak prints on the exposure of sunlight for 6 days by the change of lipid composition on the fingerprint residue with time [42,43] and pre-adsorption of dust particles on the fingerprint residue, which leaves only a lesser space for the Fuller’s earth to be adsorbed. Hence, it may be a challenge to reveal latent fingerprints using the Fuller’s earth in outdoor crime scenes. Fuller’s earth’s natural adsorption property and ability to draw out oil and grease from skin pores [44] could explain its selective adherence to finger ridge residues.

The ability of henna powder, a natural cosmetic, as a fingerprint powder was already discussed in the previous section. Apart from these materials, talcum powder [13,14] and baby powders [45–47] have also been reported to be efficient at revealing fingerprint patterns. Morgan et al. [47] applied a cornstarch-based baby powder on the extremely darkened mummified fingers to increase the visibility of the finger ridges. The baby powder-treated finger is then photographed to digitally extract the fingerprint. The authors also did the same experiment with the aid of pure cornstarch instead of the baby powder and obtained similar results. Hence, the active material in the baby powder should be cornstarch.

Minerals are composed of inorganic materials. It is reported [48] that the photostability of inorganic materials is far better than that of organic materials. Hence, the use of minerals as fingerprint developing agents opens up the possibility of exposing fingerprints stained by them for a long time to various high energy forensic light sources. Under the same conditions, organic compounds are most susceptible to photodegradation.

Garg et al. [49] studied the effectiveness of white cement (commonly used to fix floor tiles) as a fingerprint powder and compared it to a conventional white powder. Lime and silica are the major constituents of the white cement [50–52]. It differs from grey cement in terms of the oxides ofchromium, manganese, iron, copper, vanadium, nickel and titanium (which imparts the grey colour to the cement). White cement is finer than grey cement, which is an important prerequisite for a fingerprint powder. The white cement was found to be superior in developing latent fingerprints when compared to conventional white powder. On the application of white cement to twenty different substrates (Entry 1 in Table 5), fourteen substrates developed clear fingerprints. The research group also investigated the influence of ageing of fingerprints on its development using white cement on four different substrates. The maximum ageing time was forty days. Even after forty days, the white cement developed very clear fingerprints deposited on carbon paper, whereas the conventional white powder did not.

Dhunna et al. [23] investigated the efficiency of various food powders and came to the conclusion that limestone is one of the best materials for fingerprint applications. The basic constitution of limestone is calcium carbonate (CaCO3). It contains about 50% of carbonate minerals, either dolomite (calcium magnesium carbonate, CaMg [CO3]2) or calcite (pure CaCO3) or both.

Singh et al. [53] introduced waste marble slurry powder as a fingerprint developing material on various porous as well as non-porous surfaces. The material consists of 99% calcium carbonate [54]. The developed fingerprints are relatively clear to visualize. The authors state that the decipherment of latent fingerprints on the application of the marble slurry powder is due to its combining capacity with the oil and fatty acids present in the sweat of the fingerprints.

Singh et al. [57] was demonstrated the application of rock phosphate powder as a fingerprint developer on ten different porous and non-porous surfaces (Entry 5 in Table 5). Rock phosphate or phosphorite is a non-detrital sedimentary rockthat contains high amounts ofphosphate minerals in the form of fluorapatite Ca5(PO4)3F and hydroxyapatiteCa5(PO4)3OH or Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2. Aside from that, depending on the type, the phosphate rocks contain 4% to more than 30% phosphorous pentoxide (P2O5). The rock phosphate powder developed fair fingerprints on plastic and paper substrates.

83.33% successful rate out of twenty five developed prints (Entry 6 in Table 5) was reported by Chauhan et al. [29] for the fingerprints developed by vermillion, which is a brilliant red or scarlet pigment obtained from the powdered mineral cinnabar (mercury sulfide, HgS). The major issue associated with the application of vermillion as a fingerprint powder is its toxicity like many other mercury compounds.

Sharma et al. [58] applied geru (ochre) powder on latent fingermarks deposited on various non-porous smooth surfaces. Red ochre is a combination of aluminium silicate and iron oxide and it is also known as “Geru” in Ayurveda. It is a reddish-brown natural hematite mineral (Fe2O3). [59] The geru powder delivered better results on fingerprints deposited on wrist watch dial, touchscreen of a mobile phone, glass, pen drive, and plastic cards. Conversely, negative results were obtained for the fingerprints deposited on the wash bottle and brass lock.

In another study, a commonly available, cheap laboratory reagent silica gel G (TLC grade) was successfully employed by Singh et al. [60] as a fingerprint powder on various surfaces of different nature. Silica gel is a most common reagent in laboratories and is essential for chromatography techniques such as column chromatography and thin layer chromatography (TLC), where it serves as an inert stationary phase. Silica gel G is nothing but silicon dioxide (SiO2) with a binder, which is usually gypsum (CaSO4·2H2O). Silica adopts a covalent network-like structure with many hydroxyl functional groups, which facilitates differential adsorption of various substances of different polarity. Singh et al. [60] took advantage of this property of silica to use this inexpensive material in forensic fingerprint analysis. In fingerprint science, many research groups have reported silica in nano form as a carrier cum base material for dyes and pigments such as ethidium bromide [61], sodium fluorescein [61], rhodamine B [61,62], crystal violet [61], rhodamine 6G [61,62], bromothymol blue [61], PR254 [63], RuBpy [62,64], nanocarbon [65], fluorescein isothiocyanate, and so on [66]. As the surface-to-volume ratio increases when a material converted to its nano form from its bulk form, silica nanoparticles blend well with other dyes. However, Singh et al. [60] applied neat silica powder and achieved the decipherment of latent fingerprints in good contrast. The discussion of the non-conventional materials in nano form is beyond the scope of this review.

A United States patent owned by the inventor Molina [67] details the application of silica powder and talc (Mg3Si4O10(OH)2) mixed in various ratios along with a fluorescent dye such as Morton Fluorescent Yellow G and Calcofluor White RW or a daylight dye such as Oil Red O and Sudan Red O. The developed fingerprints had good contrast and visible minutiae features.

The effectiveness of silica gel G in developing the latent fingerprints inspired Ramanan and Nirmala [68] to study the applicability of neutral alumina gel G (TLC grade) in revealing latent fingerprints. Alumina gel is another commonly used laboratory reagent like silica gel. Twenty various porous and non-porous surfaces were employed in this study as the adsorbents of latent fingerprints. The alumina-developed latent fingerprints performed well on most of the surfaces tested, with the exception of currency paper and jewellery cloth box, which had a poor contrast. Microscopic image of the alumina-developed latent fingerprint exhibited an excellent contrast between the ridges and the background as shown in Fig. 22 . The Fuller’s earth, another mineral has already been discussed in the previous section.

Natural carbon materials such as graphite, activated charcoal, coke, lamp black, and carbon black have been used as an important constituent in conventional fingerprint formulations (mostly in black and grey powders) for a long time [21,69,70]. Various carbon-based conventional fingerprint powders were listed by Ciuksza [71]. Neat graphite powder as a fingerprint powder is especially useful for the demonstration of forensic fingerprinting technique to school children [14]. Hence, it is clear that the carbon-based materials can serve as efficient candidates for black fingerprint powders.

A Master of Science thesis submitted by Sangkaew [72] on the production and evaluation of fingerprint powder made from coal, another important form of natural carbon. The author introduced rosin dye to the coal to enhance the sharpness of the developed prints. The resulting fingerprints seem to be promising for practical applications.

A carbon residue could easily be obtained from any carbonaceous material by heat treatment. The resulting carbon materials vary in their compositions depending upon their precursor materials. The following lines will go over some of such non-conventional carbon materials that have been reported in the literature.

Tiprong [73] produced three types of black powders for fingerprint applications using wood charcoals obtained from the woods of rubber, jackfruit, and mangrove and applied to the surfaces of ceramic, plastic, and paper cups. The developed fingerprints clearly revealed the ridge details.

Activated carbon was prepared from banana peel by Mopoung et al. [74] and successfully applied to detect latent fingerprints on non-porous surfaces. The authors further report that the addition of 0.2% or even slightly less methylene blue intensifies the sharpness and darkness of resulting fingerprints.

Seerat et al. [22] produced carbon powders by heating marigold flowers and mustard oil and applied them as a black fingerprint developer on various surfaces. Mustard oil soot was used to make the carbon powder. The fingerprints developed by marigold powder were clearly visible on the surfaces of study except for glass and plastic surfaces. On the other hand, the mustard oil soot developed excellent fingerprints on all the surfaces investigated. The author suggests that the specific nature of mustard oil soot stems from its small size. The authors compared the fingerprints developed by mustard oil soot to charcoal-developed prints and found that mustard oil soot selectively adsorbs on the fingerprint residues whereas the charcoal adsorbed on the background also. Soot is an interesting material in forensic examination. A trained field forensic examiner can realize that soot deposits can reveal many things in a forensic investigation. For example, the fingermarks can be developed by the soot and heat in an arson scene [75–78]. Hence, a forensic examiner should perform a careful visual examination of the items retrieved from arson scenes.

Low et al. [79] developed an acid-modified Imperata cylindrica (IC) powder and studied its efficacy in developing latent fingerprints of eccrine, sebaceous, and natural types deposited on different surfaces. The authors also investigated the applicability of the acid-modified IC powder on various aged fingerprints and fingerprints that had been submerged in water for different time periods. The corresponding fingerprints are shown in Fig. 23 . The fingerprints developed by acid-modified IC powder provide good visibility. However, it did not outdistance the efficiency of conventional Sirchie® black powder. The possible reason suggested by the authors for the selective staining of finger ridge emanations was the hydrogen bond formation between the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups of the powder and the fatty acids or glycerides of sebum.

Thongthammachad et al. [80] have prepared a fingerprint powder from heat-treated black rice husk and applied it for forensic fingerprint detection. The black rice husk is a major waste produced in rice mills and consists mainly of silica and organic matter. The organic matter in turn consists of approximately 51% carbon, 42% oxygen, and the rest are hydrogen and nitrogen. Silica is found mostly around the surface of the husk. The results obtained for black rice husk powder are comparable to those obtained for conventional powder. On the other hand, carbon nanoparticles were prepared from rice husk and utilized as fingerprint powders by Rajan et al. [81,82] which is out of the scope of this review.

The discussion of carbon-based fingerprint powders would be incomplete without a few words about carbon quantum dots (or simply carbon dots) which are nanoparticles of carbon with sizes less than 10 nm. Their diverse properties, such as high luminescence quantum yield, photostability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, up-converted luminescence, long luminescence lifetime, various surface functional groups, possibilities for surface modifications, facile synthesis, electron donor–acceptor property, and so on, make them very good candidates for various applications, such as luminescence sensors, biomarkers, drug delivery, nanomedicine, catalysts, optronics, energy conversion and storage, and so on [83–87]. An interesting thing about these carbon dots is that they can easily be synthesized not only from organic/inorganic molecules, materials, polymers, and so on, but also from waste materials such as eutrophic algal blooms [18], jack fruit seeds [19], goat hooves [88], thermocol [89], and so on [90]. Moreover, their photostability is superior compared to organic molecules such as rhodamine 6G. Few research groups [91–98] have reported preliminary results on the efficiency of carbon dots (bare carbon dots or carbon dots along with another material) as luminescent fingerprint materials, which are detailed in a recent review by Kanodarwala et al. [20]. As the review article summarizes, no work reported so far on the dedicated application of carbon dots as fingerprint developer. The reported articles are mainly focused on the synthesis, characterization and photophysical behavior of the carbon dots. The fingerprint application is simply portrayed as one of the potential applications of carbon dots. Dedicated research works focused on the decipherment of latent fingerprints of various types such as eccrine, sebaceous, and natural deposited on various porous as well as non-porous surfaces using these novel carbon-based nanomaterials is the urgent need. The efficiency of these materials should also be tested on aged, submerged, buried, and burnt fingerprints and compared to conventional fingerprint powders.

Kumari et al. [16] applied festival colors to reveal latent fingerprints. The festival color powders (holi powder or gulal or abir) are famous in some Asian populations like India, particularly during the celebration of “holi” festival. In ancient times, people used natural sources like turmeric powder, neem powder, sandalwood powder, rose powder, etc. as festival colors to celebrate the holi festival [99,100]. Later, due to scarcity of natural resources and the rise of corporate companies, the natural colors were replaced with synthetic colors such as copper sulfate (for green), mercury sulfide (for red), chromium iodide (for purple), aluminium bromide (for silver), Prussian blue (for blue), lead oxide (for black), rhodamine B (for pink), auramine (for yellow), and so on [99,100]. The natural base material, corn starch, which was used to dilute the festival colors, was also replaced with talc, asbestos, and other chemicals. However, still, some people stick with natural colors. Since the authors bought the festival colors in a local market, it is difficult to know about their compositions. The authors employed the green, red, and pink holi colors on the sweat and sebaceous fingerprints deposited on various surfaces such as aluminium foil and compact disc. The resulting fingerprints clearly show their important identification features.

Later, in 2018, Jain et al. [101] used “rangoli” powders to develop latent fingerprints deposited on various surfaces such as glass, polished iron plate, deodorant can, tile, and lock under wet conditions. Rangoli powders are color powders used to make rangoli, an art that originated in the Indian subcontinent in which different patterns are drawn on the ground during festivals [102]. The authors stated that the major constituents of the rangoli powders they employed are calcium and silica, with various minor substituents. The latent fingerprints are clearly developed on the surfaces being examined. However, aged (1 day) fingerprints deposited on glass surfaces were not visible on the application of rangoli powders.

A new fingerprint powder should be tested on various surfaces of different natures to determine its practical applicability [36,103]. In this sense, the turmeric powder (tested on twenty six different surfaces), white cement (tested on twenty two different surfaces), neutral alumina and silica gel G (tested on twenty different surfaces) applied well on diverse surfaces, providing readers with a thorough understanding of them. Next to that, cornflour, custard powder, and baking powder were tested on seventeen different surfaces while cocoa powder, baking soda, black salt, and edible food colour (orange) were tested on fourteen different substrates.

In addition, any new potential fingerprint powder must be compared to existing “successful” currently utilized powders - typically using split depletion prints to make direct comparisons [36,103]. Among the various non-conventional powders discussed in this review, split depletion studies were carried out only for acid-modified IC powder [78] and algal biomass powders [17]. Whereas the comparisons of fingerprints developed using non-conventional powders to conventional fingerprint powders were studied for banana peel-activated carbon, turmeric powder, coal powder, white cement, non-conventional household food powders [28], NIR powders [30], and multani mitti.

Out of the sixty four various commonly available, non-conventional fingerprint materials that are included in this review, turmeric powder seems to be a promising material. It develops clear prints on different surfaces of different nature. However, it fails on flexible surfaces such as skin and rubber, as well as porous absorbing materials like cardboard. These surfaces are always challenging to forensic fingerprinting. Apart from turmeric powder, white cement, silica gel G, neutral alumina, multani mitti, Robin® powder blue, food colors, and Spirulina platensis powder are also giving new hopes. It could be noted that good quality fingerprints are developed by starch-based non-conventional powders such as gram flour, wheat flour, cornflour, and bleach activator. The dull appearance of the fingerprints developed by another starch-based non-conventional powder, durian seed powder could be due to the application method adopted by the author or the wrong angle of photographs. It is understood that chilli-based non-conventional powders such as red chilli powder and paprika powder are not suitable for fingerprint applications. Outcomes of this discussion section are summarized below in Table 1 .

The applications of commonly available, everyday materials to reveal the latent fingerprints are comprehensively reviewed in this article. Tables 2–7
given below, summarize the specifics of non-conventional fingerprint materials discussed in this review, as a ready reference for the reader’s convenience.

The number of non-conventional powders presented in this review is not exhaustive. More commonly available everyday materials should be examined on several surfaces and on several conditions to explore their suitability as non-conventional fingerprint powders. Hence, this field is wide open to researchers. So far, the new emerging luminescent nanomaterial, carbon dots has not been satisfactorily investigated as a dedicative fingerprint powder. Their ease of synthesis, very good luminescent quantum yields, stability under various environments, and environmentally benign nature, uphold their fitness as a fingerprint powder and compelresearchers, especially those with forensic backgrounds, to invest their interests in them as they deserve it.
